actions.thought: In a three-handed game, the button is a highly advantageous position. K4s is a suited hand with a King, which holds reasonable equity to open the pot and take control. Opening to 2.5x the big blind is a standard approach to thin the field and potentially pick up the blinds, while keeping the pot manageable if called.

Jh 8c is a weak offsuit hand to play from the small blind against a button open. I am out of position for the rest of the hand, and this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent move.

ATo is a solid hand defending the Big Blind against a Button open. While a 3-bet could be viable, calling is standard as it keeps the Button's wider opening range, including weaker aces and dominated broadway hands, in the pot. The pot odds are favorable, so I will call and evaluate the flop out of position.

The flop 5h 6d 7c connects very well with the preflop raiser's range, hitting many middle pairs, straight draws, and two pairs. I completely missed this flop with Ace-Ten high and have no strong draws. Being out of position against the preflop raiser, the standard and most prudent play is to check and evaluate his action.

The board texture 5h 6d 7c is highly connected and hits the Big Blind's calling range significantly harder than my opening range. With only King-high and a weak gutshot draw, betting into a board that favors my opponent's range is high-variance and likely to be met with a check-raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings a complete blank with the 2s. Since Flashlight checked behind on the highly coordinated flop, his range is likely capped at marginal showdown value hands, such as weak pairs, or unpaired overcards that missed the board. My Big Blind defending range connects very well with this 5-6-7 board, as I can represent many combinations of pairs, two pairs, and straights. By making a turn probe bet, I can put pressure on his stronger Ace-high hands (like AK, AQ, AJ) and Broadway combinations to fold, potentially taking down the pot uncontested.

The board is 5h 6d 7c 2s, and I hold Ks 4s. I have a gutshot straight draw, needing an 8 or 3 for a straight. The opponent's bet of 300 into an 850 pot provides favorable odds (roughly 3:1) to continue. As I am in position, calling allows me to realize my equity and see the river card. Raising would be a speculative bluff, and folding is too tight given the drawing potential.

The 4d on the river puts a four-card straight (4-5-6-7) on the board. My Ace-Ten high has almost no showdown value, as Flashlight's turn call indicates he has at least a marginal pair or a straight draw, which has now likely improved or still beats Ace-high. While I could attempt a large bluff representing an 8 or a 3 to force folds from single-pair hands, Flashlight's range contains many combinations of 8x and 3x (like 87, 88, A8, or A3) that he would have called with on the turn. Attempting a high-variance bluff on this heavily coordinated board in the very first hand of the tournament is an unnecessary risk. The most disciplined play is to check and surrender the pot, intending to fold to any bet.

The board texture is extremely connected (2-4-5-6-7), making a straight possible for anyone holding a 3 or an 8. My pair of 4s has very low showdown value and is easily beaten by a wide range of hands, including any higher pair or a straight. Since Pro checked to me on the river, betting would only serve to bloat the pot when I am likely behind or induce a fold from hands that I am already beating, while getting called by hands that crush me. Checking back is the most prudent course of action to reach showdown cheaply and avoid unnecessary risk.

I am on the button with Q9s, which is a strong starting hand for this position. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the blinds and capitalize on my positional advantage. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 chips.

I am in the Small Blind with J9o, which is a weak offsuit holding. The Button opened to 3 big blinds. Calling here out of position with a marginal hand is a losing play, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (6-3 offsuit). Calling a button raise from out of position with such a disconnected low-card hand is mathematically disadvantageous. There is no incentive to defend my blind against a standard raise size with this specific holding.
Holding 10-4 offsuit on the button, this is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity. It's an easy fold pre-flop to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the small blind with 5-6 offsuit. The button has folded, leaving a heads-up pot against the big blind. Completing the small blind is a standard strategy here given the pot odds and the connectivity of my hand, allowing me to see a flop with a decent range.

I have 7d 4d in the Big Blind. Flashlight in the Small Blind has completed the bet, and the Button has folded. Since I can see the flop for free, there is no need to raise with a marginal hand like 74 suited. I will check to see the flop.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (5-6 offsuit) on an Ace-high board (A-J-8). With no pairs, no flush draws, and no straight draws, I have virtually no equity. Checking is the only sensible option to see if my opponent, who is in position, wants to bet, but I will prepare to fold if there is any aggression.

I have 7d 4d on an Ah Jc 8h board. I have no pair and no real draws. Flashlight checked to me in a limped pot. There is no reason to bluff here as the board likely hits a limping range better than my specific hand, and I can take a free card to try and improve.

I am holding 5-6 offsuit on an 8-J-A-K board. This is a very poor texture for my range, and my specific hand has essentially zero showdown value and no remaining draw potential. Since I am first to act on the turn, I must check. Betting here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed given the board hits the opponent's defending range heavily, so checking to re-evaluate is the only standard play.

I have 7-high with no draws on a board of As Jc 8h Kh. My opponent (Flashlight) has checked for the second time. While the King is a scare card that I could potentially represent, the pot is small and I have zero showdown value or equity to improve. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize any miracle improvement or fold to a bet on the river without losing more chips now.

I hold 5-6 offsuit and have completely missed the board, leaving me with only 5-high. The action has been checked through on both the flop and turn, suggesting neither player has a strong hand, but betting into the opponent here is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that has showdown value, and it would only bloat the pot while I am behind. Checking is the standard play to reach the showdown with no risk of further loss.

I have 7-high on a board of A-J-8-K-K. Flashlight has checked on every street in this limped pot, indicating a very weak range. However, 7-high has zero showdown value. While I could attempt a bluff to win the small 200-chip pot, the board is saturated with high cards (A, K, J) that likely hit any random cards Flashlight might be holding. Since the pot is so small and I am in a long-term tournament, I will check and concede the hand rather than risking chips on a low-probability bluff.

Holding Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor potential for post-flop equity, even when holding the button in a three-handed game. It lacks the strength to call or raise profitably against opponents. Folding is the standard and disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 6d. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) and me. 3d 6d is a very weak hand to play out of position, even if it is suited. While I could call the additional 50 or raise to steal the blinds, against a player named 'Pro' in the Big Blind, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than risking chips with a low-equity hand in a poor position.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Js Jc) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. With a stack of 100.5 big blinds, I want to raise to build value and gain control of the pot. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here.

Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3x open from the button, I am frequently dominated by better queens, tens, or broadway combinations. The best and standard action here is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

The opponent has raised to 300 from the button. Holding 8c4c in the big blind, I am getting decent pot odds to call and see a flop. While 84s is a marginal hand, defending the big blind against a button open is standard strategy to prevent being pushed over, and the suited nature of the cards gives me some playability post-flop.

I am the pre-flop caller in the big blind and I am first to act on this flop. Holding bottom pair (four) with a backdoor flush draw, checking is the standard and most strategic play. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor with such a weak made hand would put me in a difficult spot if raised, so I will check to see how the opponent proceeds.

I have flopped the top set of Jacks on a Jh-4h-7s board. This is an incredibly strong hand. While I block some top pair combinations (like AJ or KJ), there are still many hands Flashlight can call with, including flush draws, straight draws (56, 89), or middle pairs. I want to build the pot immediately and charge any draws. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot is a standard value bet here.

The opponent has bet 400 into a pot of 650, giving me 2.6:1 pot odds. With a pair of 4s on a board of Jh-4h-7s, my hand has some showdown value against the opponent's likely range of overcards or air c-bets. Folding is too tight given the odds, and raising is inefficient as it forces the opponent to play perfectly against me. Calling is the most disciplined approach to see if I can improve or if the opponent slows down on later streets.

The Ace of Clubs on the turn is a very strong card for the pre-flop aggressor's range, which likely includes many Ace-X broadway hands. My pair of 4s is now significantly weaker and serves only as a bluff catcher. With a weak pair on a board that has become much more dangerous, the most strategic play is to check, control the size of the pot, and re-evaluate based on the opponent's action. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause a raise that forces me to fold, so checking is the most disciplined approach.

I have a set of Jacks on a board of Jh-4h-7s-Ac. This is an extremely strong hand. The Ace on the turn is generally a good card for my range as the pre-flop raiser, but it also might have given Flashlight top pair if they were calling the flop with a hand like Ax of hearts or a floating Ace. Flashlight checked to me again. I need to continue betting for value and to protect against the heart flush draw. A bet of around 60% of the pot keeps the pot building and prices out draws while getting value from an Ace or a Jack.

The opponent is firing a second barrel of 900 into the pot on an Ace-high turn, which strongly suggests they have connected with the board, likely holding an Ace or better. My pair of fours is essentially a bluff catcher, and in this position, it is far too weak to continue against continued aggression. Calling would be a losing play against the opponent's value-heavy betting range on this texture. The most disciplined and strategic decision is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Holding J8o on the Button 3-handed. This is a weak offsuit gapper and not strong enough to open-raise for value or as a high-EV steal. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack for better spots.

My starting hand of 2c 6s is extremely weak with poor playability. Even though I have already posted the 50-chip small blind, investing additional capital into a hand with such low potential is mathematically negative expected value. It is more prudent to fold, accept the loss of the small blind, and wait for a stronger starting hand to protect my stack.

With 4-5 offsuit, the hand has very little equity and does not warrant an open-raise from the button. Given my stack size and the goal of maximizing long-term profitability, folding is the most disciplined and statistically sound decision to avoid unnecessary risks with a weak holding.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 4s, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it has folded to me and I could theoretically attempt to steal the Big Blind, 3-4 offsuit has very little playability post-flop if called. Given the professional level of the opponent in the Big Blind, I prefer to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than committing more chips with one of the worst possible starting hands.

I am on the button with Ten of Clubs and Three of Spades. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop. Although I have a slight chip lead and being on the button allows for wider opening ranges, T3o is too weak to justify a raise or a call in this spot. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity.
J7o is a weak hand to play from the small blind, especially being out of position against the big blind post-flop. The best play is to simply fold and preserve the stack.

65s is a premium suited connector that plays very well in position. Being on the button, raising gives me the chance to steal the blinds immediately or play a pot with the initiative and positional advantage if called. I'll raise to 2.5x (250).

Queen-Jack offsuit is a reasonably strong hand to defend from the small blind against a button open. While I will be out of position, the pot odds (getting 2:1 on a call) are favorable. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and maintain a manageable stack-to-pot ratio, rather than committing to a 3-bet out of position against an active opener.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 2d, one of the weakest starting hands. Pro raised from the button to 250 and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 600), this hand performs very poorly multi-way and out of position. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

The flop of K-8-9 favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. With only queen-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have very little equity to bet for value or semi-bluff against a likely king in my opponent's range. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot manageable and see if I can improve on the turn or take a free card if the opponent checks back.

With 6s5s on an 8c Kh 9d flop, I have a weak gutshot straight draw. The K-9-8 board connects reasonably well with the small blind's pre-flop calling range, which likely includes many King-x broadways, suited connectors like JT/T9, and medium pocket pairs. Since my draw is to the low end and I lack fold equity against that range, checking back to take a free card is the safest play.

With Queen-Jack on a K-9-8-2 board, I have a gutshot straight draw and some overcard potential, but currently I am behind most hands that would call a bet. Since the flop checked through, checking the turn is the most standard line to control the pot size and realize my equity for free, as betting likely only gets called by better hands while forcing out hands I might beat on the river if I hit or if the board runs out cleanly.

Flashlight's double check indicates a lack of interest in this board, heavily discounting strong Kx hands. This makes it a great spot for a delayed c-bet. Betting half the pot puts pressure on his A-highs, missed broadway cards, and small pocket pairs. Even if called, I still retain a little bit of equity with my gutshot straight draw.

I am facing a half-pot bet of 300 into a 600 pot. My hand, Queen-Jack offsuit, has only a gutshot straight draw (requiring a Ten) and Q-high, which is likely behind the opponent's range given the K-high board. Mathematically, I am being offered 3:1 pot odds, requiring 25% equity to call profitably. With only 4 outs, my direct equity is approximately 8.7%, which does not justify the call. Without sufficient implied odds to cover the deficit, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

I am on the button in a three-handed game. With King-Four suited, I have a reasonable hand to open the pot and try to take down the blinds. A raise to 250 is a standard sizing to apply pressure and utilize my positional advantage.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) is a decent hand, but being in the Small Blind is the worst positional disadvantage. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 2.5x, which is a standard opening range that often contains better Aces or pocket pairs. While I could 3-bet to attempt to take the pot down, A7o doesn't play well post-flop if called, especially out of position. Calling would also give the Big Blind (Pro) a great price to enter the pot, creating a multi-way scenario where my hand's value decreases significantly. Given the early stage of the tournament and my current chip lead, I'll opt for a disciplined fold to avoid unnecessary variance and wait for a more advantageous position or a stronger hand.

A5o is a weak offsuit Ace that plays poorly out of position. Against a 2.5x raise from the button, A5o is generally a fold or an aggressive 3-bet bluff, but calling leads to tough post-flop spots where we could be dominated. Given the depth and early stage of the match, folding to preserve chips for better positional advantages or stronger hands is the most sensible choice.

I am on the button with Ten of Hearts and Six of Spades (T6o). In a three-handed game, this hand is quite weak and sits at the bottom of a potential opening range. Given my current chip lead and the fact that this hand performs poorly post-flop, I will choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). This is a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand with very poor playability post-flop. Even though the Button folded and I could attempt a steal, doing so out of position with a trash hand is a losing long-term play. The best decision is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

On the Button with an offsuit weak connector (8c 7d). While we have position, 87o is too weak to try to steal the blinds profitably, especially since both blinds have full stacks and we want to preserve our stack for better hands.

In the small blind with KTo against only the big blind, I have a significant advantage in equity. Raising allows me to take down the pot immediately or isolate the big blind, where KTo performs well heads-up. Raising to 300 is a standard size to put pressure on the opponent and establish control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300, which is a 3x open. ATo is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle and is well ahead of the average Small Blind raising range. Since I will have position throughout the rest of the hand, I will call to see a flop. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a range that might contain stronger Aces.

I have flopped top pair with a strong Ten kicker on a board that is relatively dry, though it does offer some backdoor flush potential with the two spades. My opponent in the big blind has a wide range, so betting for value and protection is the correct play. A bet of 300 into the 600 pot (half-pot) is appropriate to define the hand strength and discourage free cards for draws.

I have middle pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). On a board of K-T-2, my hand is strong enough to call a single continuation bet from the Small Blind in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Raising might overplay the hand and isolate me against better hands like Kings or sets, while folding is far too tight. Calling allows me to maintain pot control and utilize my positional advantage on the turn.

The board has become highly coordinated with the Queen of Hearts on the turn, opening up possibilities for straights (specifically any Jack). While I have two pair (Kings and Tens), which is a strong hand, it is vulnerable on this texture. Betting here could alienate me against hands I beat while inviting raises from better hands like sets or already made straights. Checking is the most prudent approach to pot control, allowing me to see the river and evaluate my opponent's action without over-committing to a potentially dangerous board.

The Queen on the turn is a difficult card for my hand, as it is now another overcard to my pair of Tens. When Flashlight checks, they might be slowing down with a medium hand, giving up on a bluff, or occasionally trapping. Since the board is quite coordinated with straight and flush possibilities, betting into a checked opponent might isolate me against better hands like Kings or Queens. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control, protect my showdown value, and see a river card for free.

The river Nine of Clubs completes potential straight combinations (9-T-J-Q-K) for any player holding a Jack. My two pair, Kings and Tens, is now quite vulnerable, as I lose to any Jack, any better two pair (such as Kings and Queens), and any set. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, while checking allows me to get to showdown safely. Since the turn was checked through, it is unlikely my opponent is folding a worse hand that they would have bet anyway, and they may be checking down a hand with marginal showdown value. Checking is the optimal play to control the pot size and reach showdown.

I have a pair of Tens with an Ace kicker on a very coordinated board (K-Q-T-9-2). Flashlight raised pre-flop and bet the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically indicates a marginal hand that is looking to get to showdown cheaply or a missed draw. While I have showdown value, the board is dangerous because any Jack makes a straight, and many hands containing a King or Queen also beat me. By checking back, I can see the showdown for free and minimize risk, as it's unlikely a worse hand would call a bet on this board texture.

Ace-Nine suited is a strong holding in the button position, especially in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise allows me to take the initiative and potentially pick up the blinds, or play the hand in position post-flop with a strong range advantage.

Q9o is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a 2.5x open from the Button. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand makes this a difficult spot to realize equity. Given the early stage of the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for better opportunities.
72o is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, I have absolutely no equity to defend or play this hand, so folding is the only viable option.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and want to exert pressure on the blinds to either take the pot immediately or play a pot in position. A standard 3x open to 300 is consistent with my previous play and appropriate for the current blinds.

We are 3-handed. Flash raises to 3x from the Button. While A7o is relatively strong in a 3-max game, calling from the Small Blind is unappealing because we will be out of position post-flop and the Big Blind could squeeze behind us. A 3-bet bluff is an option, but A7o lacks the suitedness to make it a prime candidate. Folding is the most solid and disciplined decision to avoid playing a tricky post-flop situation out of position with a marginal offsuit Ace.

Holding 5-7 offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity and playing out of position with such a weak holding is strategically unsound. It is best to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

Q8 offsuit is a weak hand to open from the Button, even 3-handed. It is often dominated by hands that will call or 3-bet from the blinds. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve the stack.

My hand (Jh, 4s) is extremely weak, and as the small blind, I am out of position against the big blind. Given the early stage of the tournament, it is strategically sound to preserve my stack rather than contest a pot with a marginal hand that will likely be dominated.

My hand, King-Three offsuit, is too weak to play from the button. Even in a three-handed game, this hand lacks sufficient value to warrant a raise or a call against the blinds, so folding is the disciplined choice to conserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Tens (Tc, Ts), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The Button has folded, so I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I should raise to build the pot and exert pressure. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to capture value and potentially win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop advantage.

Q5o is a very weak hand, especially against a 3x open raise from the small blind. I am out of position post-flop if I call, and my hand lacks the equity and playability to defend profitably. The best decision is to fold.

I am on the Button with Ace-Five offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage against the blinds. I will open for 300 (3x the big blind), which has been a consistent sizing in this game.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, QTo is generally too weak to call out of position from the Small Blind. The threat of the Big Blind squeezing, combined with the disadvantage of playing a marginal unsuited broadway hand out of position post-flop, makes folding the most solid option.
I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (5-2 offsuit). Defending this hand against a button raise would be unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack.

96o is a very weak and unplayable hand pre-flop. Even though I am on the button, it is not worth investing chips with such a disconnected and offsuit hand. The best decision is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

Pro has folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. With King-high (Kd9d) and a positional advantage as the small blind, raising is the optimal move to put pressure on the opponent and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds and would have position post-flop, Q3o is a very weak hand that is easily dominated and flops poorly. Given the tournament context and the fact that I've been playing conservatively, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against an active opener. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

7-4 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with poor connectivity and low equity. Even in a short-handed game where the button position is advantageous, there is no strategic justification for investing chips with these cards. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 7s. The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. 57o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. While I could attempt a steal or complete the bet for 50 more, the hand's lack of high-card value and poor post-flop playability make it a clear fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite having positional advantage for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to commit chips to the pot with such a weak holding. Folding is the standard professional play here to preserve stack size for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak, unsuited hand (4c 3d). The Button has folded, but playing out of position against the Big Blind with such poor cards is a losing long-term strategy. The correct move is to simply fold.

Being first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game, our opening range is relatively wide. Queen-Seven suited is definitely strong enough to open-raise here to steal the blinds or play in position post-flop. I'll make a standard raise of 2.5 big blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when facing a raise from the button. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against an aggressive player. Given the pot odds and positional disadvantage, folding is the disciplined and optimal play to conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 3s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. Although the price to call is relatively low (150 more to win a total pot of 550), 63o is an extremely weak hand and playing it out of position against the button is likely to lead to a loss. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

75 suited is a playable hand from the button position in a three-handed game. I have been playing somewhat tight, so opening the pot with a raise is a standard move to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds, leveraging my position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 7c, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. Given my position and the lack of strength in my hand, calling or raising is not profitable. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

Facing a 2.5x open from the Button, QTo is a perfectly reasonable hand to defend from the Big Blind. The pot odds are favorable (150 to win 400, needing about 27.2% equity), and the hand can flop well with straight draws and strong pairs. We will call and navigate post-flop.

On a paired Ace board (Ac Qs As), the pre-flop raiser's range has a significant advantage, as it contains many strong Ax hands like AK, AQ, AJ, AT, etc. I have bottom pair (Queens) which serves as a marginal bluff-catcher. As the out-of-position pre-flop caller, checking to the raiser is the standard play. Leading out (donk betting) into this board would be a mistake.

The flop (Ac Qs As) is extremely dry and heavily favors my pre-flop opening range. As the aggressor, a continuation bet is the standard play here. It is unlikely that my opponent has connected with this board, so a bet of 250 into the 550 pot should be sufficient to take down the pot or force a fold from hands that missed.

Facing a small continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 550, my pair of Queens acts as a solid bluff-catcher. On this paired A-A-Q board with two spades, the pre-flop raiser will be c-betting a very wide range, including spade flush draws, underpairs, and pure air. While I am currently behind any Ax and better Qx holdings, the favorable pot odds (getting over 3 to 1) and the fact that I am ahead of a large portion of their c-betting bluffs make this a standard call on the flop to defend my big blind range. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

The King of Hearts on the turn is a terrible card for my hand. It heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which contains many Ax and Kx combinations. My pair of Queens is now effectively counterfelted against any King, as my opponent would have Aces and Kings. Out of position with a marginal bluff-catcher that has lost significant value, the standard play is to check to control the pot size and re-evaluate if facing a bet.

I have absolutely no equity on this board (Ace, Queen, Ace, King). My opponent called my flop bet, which suggests they likely have a piece of this board (likely a Queen, a King, or perhaps even an Ace if they are slow-playing, though unlikely given the texture). Betting again would be a pure bluff into a range that is sticky on this board. Since I have 7-high, I have zero showdown value, but checking behind is the safest play to keep the pot controlled and potentially evaluate on the river or give up if they lead out.

On the river, our pair of Queens (giving us Aces and Queens with a King kicker) has marginal showdown value. The turn checked through, indicating the opponent likely doesn't have a strong Ax hand and might be pot controlling a Kx, Qx, or an underpair, or simply giving up on a draw. We do not have enough equity to value bet, as a bet will mostly be called by better hands like Kx or Ax, and worse hands will fold. Checking allows us to either see a cheap showdown against their marginal holdings or re-evaluate if they decide to bluff their missed draws.

I have already reached the river with seven-high, which has no showdown value. On the river, the board (2s, Tc, Qd, 6c, 7c) provides a lot of possibilities for my opponent to have connected. My pocket nines are effectively a bluff catcher at this point, as any Queen, Ten, Seven, or Six has me beaten. Pro checked the river after betting the turn, which could be a sign of a missed draw, a weak hand giving up, or a trap. Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands, and checking behind allows me to reach showdown safely with my modest pair without risking further chips against a potential trap.

K3o is a weak hand even in a 3-handed game. While the button is a good position to steal, K3o lacks playability and is easily dominated. I'll wait for a stronger hand or a better spot to apply pressure.

With A9 suited in the small blind and the button having folded, this is a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind encounter. Raising is the best option to either steal the big blind immediately or build the pot with a hand that plays very well post-flop. A standard raise to 3 big blinds is a solid play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c9h, a very weak hand. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Given that 7-9 offsuit has poor equity against almost any raising range and playing out of position against an aggressive opponent is disadvantageous, the best strategic move to preserve my stack is to fold.

Holding Ten-Three offsuit on the Button is far too weak to consider opening, even when trying to steal the blinds. The hand lacks connectivity, suit, and high card value. The most profitable action is to simply fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, Queen-Eight offsuit. Given the position and hand strength, there is little incentive to contest the pot against the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined and conservative play here.

My hole cards are 3c 2s, which is a very weak hand with no equity. Even though I am on the button, there is no reason to play such a trash hand. Folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack.

K2o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an active opponent in the Big Blind. While King-high has some showdown value, the potential for being dominated or facing aggression post-flop makes folding the most prudent choice to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Ace-Six offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in this position. I have been consistently raising to 300 in late position with my opening range to maintain a balanced strategy and apply pressure to the blinds. A6o has decent equity against the blinds' defending ranges and I will have positional advantage post-flop.

Flash is opening frequently from the button with a size of 300. King-Ten suited is a strong holding that plays very well as a 3-bet from the small blind. By 3-betting, I can punish his wide button opening range, deny equity to the big blind, and take the betting initiative going into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. The Button raised, and the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 1000. While AQo is strong, 3-betting from the Small Blind against a Button open indicates a strong range from Pro. Folding is too tight, and 4-betting might bloat the pot unnecessarily out of position, risking a shove from a strong range. Calling 900 more to see a flop is a balanced approach that maintains pot odds and allows me to re-evaluate on the flop.

I opened A6o from the button, but the situation changed significantly when the Small Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 1000 and the Big Blind (Flashlight) cold-called. A6o is significantly behind the ranges typically involved in a 3-bet pot, especially a multi-way one. I am likely dominated by stronger Aces or facing pocket pairs. Even with the positional advantage and decent pot odds, the risk of losing a much larger portion of my stack post-flop is too high. Folding is the disciplined play here.

The flop of Ks Qd Jh is highly coordinated and heavily favors a cold-caller's range, which likely includes strong broadways like AK, AQ, KQ, AT, and middle to high pairs (QQ, JJ). I hold Kd Td, giving me top pair, an open-ended straight draw, and a backdoor flush draw. Despite having substantial equity, I am likely currently behind much of Flashlight's value range. Out of position, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep his bluffs or weaker holdings in the hand. I plan to evaluate and likely check-call depending on his sizing.

The board texture is extremely coordinated (K-Q-J) and hits a 3-betting range very hard. While I have a pair of Queens and a gutshot straight draw to the broadway straight, betting into the 3-bettor (Pro) when they check to me could be playing into a trap or isolating myself against a stronger range that is checking to induce a bluff. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and re-evaluate on the turn.

After the flop checked through, the 3d on the turn significantly improves my equity, giving me a king-high flush draw to go along with my top pair and open-ended straight draw. This is a massive combo draw. Betting here takes the initiative, extracts value from worse made hands like Qx or Jx, and builds the pot for when I hit my draws. A half-pot bet of 1150 is a strong delayed continuation bet that puts pressure on marginal hands while keeping my sizing balanced.

I hold a pair of Queens with a gutshot straight draw on a coordinated board (K-Q-J-3). The opponent's check on the flop indicates they likely missed or are playing passively, and their turn bet of 1150 into a 3450 pot offers decent pot odds for me to continue. Given my solid equity, including a pair of Queens and the potential for a straight, folding is too conservative, and raising risks inflating a pot against a range that could have me beat. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see a river card to potentially improve my hand.

The river Qc pairs the board, completing potential full houses and giving any Queen trips. My two pair (Kings and Queens) is essentially a weak bluff-catcher on this board texture. Given Flashlight's preflop cold-call of a 3-bet and their call on the turn, their range is heavily weighted towards strong holdings like AK, AQ, KQ, JJ, or AT (straight), all of which beat my hand. I have no value bet here, and bluffing into a range so densely packed with premium hands and trips/full houses is extremely unprofitable. Checking to evaluate their action and likely folding to a bet is the safest and most optimal play.

I have three of a kind (Queens) after the river paired the board. The opponent checked, which often indicates a missed draw or a weaker made hand, as they would likely value bet a full house. Given my strong hand, I should bet for value to get paid by hands like King-Jack, Ace-King, or a lone King. A bet of 2000 is a standard sizing to extract value while remaining cautious of potential straights.

My two pair (Kings and Queens with a Jack kicker) is essentially a bluff-catcher that loses to the vast majority of Flashlight's value range, including AK, AQ, KQ, QJ, JJ, and AT. Given his pre-flop cold call of my 3-bet and turn call, his range is incredibly strong and weighted heavily toward hands that beat me. There are very few natural bluffs in his line that take this sizing on the river, making a call highly unprofitable. Following my river plan, folding is the disciplined and optimal decision.

I am on the Button with 9s8s, a strong suited connector. With 57 big blinds, this is an excellent hand to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a post-flop pot in position if called. A standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind is appropriate here.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Calling would put me in a disadvantaged position against the button, and potentially the big blind, with a hand that is easily dominated. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Hearts. Pro raised from the Button to 250. Defending with such a weak hand against a button opening range often leads to losing more chips on later streets. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q8o, which is a playable hand in a 3-handed game. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds, leveraging my position. A raise to 300 is a standard size that provides pressure while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Flashlight, the current chip leader, has opened for 3x from the button. While K8o has some equity against a wide button opening range, playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a deep-stacked and active opponent is disadvantageous. Given my healthy stack of over 90 big blinds, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for better spots where I have either initiative or position.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 2s, which is essentially the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I have zero playability and no equity to justify a call or a 3-bet. This is a trivial fold.

I am on the button with pocket fives in a three-handed game. This is a strong starting hand for this position. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds, following the betting patterns observed in previous hands.
98 offsuit is too weak to call a 3x raise from the small blind, especially being out of position against the button with the big blind still to act. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 4c. The Button has raised to 300. While the pot odds (calling 200 to win 450) are tempting, 74s is a very weak hand with low equity. Against a Button raise, playing this hand out of position is negative expected value. Given the tournament structure and my stack size, it is better to fold and wait for a more playable hand.
Holding 2h 4c on the button is an easy fold. It's one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em and lacks any playability or raw equity. Mucking immediately.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded. Holding Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind, I should play aggressively as the chip leader. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or continue with a range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o), which is a strong hand in a three-handed game, especially against a Small Blind open. Flashlight, the current chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind after the Button folded. A9o is well ahead of a typical Small Blind opening range. While a 3-bet is a viable option to take control of the hand, calling allows me to see a flop in position and keep the pot manageable against the big stack. The price to call is 200 to win a total pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds, which is more than sufficient for this hand.

I am holding middle pair (8s) with an Ace kicker on a coordinated board (8d, Jh, 7c). As the pre-flop aggressor, I maintain the initiative. Checking would allow the opponent to see a free card with potential overcards or straight draws. A continuation bet is standard here to protect my hand and extract value from worse pairs or weaker hands that might have called pre-flop. A sizing of 350 (just over half the pot) is appropriate to keep the pressure on while not over-committing with a marginal top-pair strength hand.

On a flop of 8d-Jh-7c, I have Ace-high with one overcard and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Flashlight's lead of 350 into a 600 pot (approx. 58% pot) is a standard continuation bet on a coordinated board. Calling allows me to see a turn card and potentially realize my equity if a Ten or an Ace (cautiously) peels off.

I have improved to top two pair (Aces and Eights) on the turn, which is a very strong hand. The Ace is a favorable card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor. The opponent called a flop bet on a board with straight draws, so they likely have a piece of the board like a Jack, a pair of Eights, or a draw. I should bet for value to get paid by weaker Jacks or second pairs while protecting against straight draws like 9-10. A bet of 700, slightly over half the pot, is appropriate to extract value and set up a final river bet.

On the turn, the Ace of Spades (As) improves my hand to top pair with a nine kicker. Flashlight's bet of 700 into a 1300 pot is a standard continuation/value bet. While I am vulnerable to better Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) or two-pair/straights, top pair is too strong to fold in a three-handed game given the pot odds of nearly 3:1. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the action on the river, especially since I am in position.

I have top two pair (Aces and Eights), which is a very strong hand on this board. The river 2d is a blank card. Since the opponent called my flop and turn bets, they likely have a hand like a Jack or an Ace with a weaker kicker. I should bet for value to get paid off by these second-best hands. A bet of 1,000 chips is reasonable, offering enough value while not polarizing my range too much to force a fold from weaker holdings.

I have top pair (Aces) with a nine kicker in a blind-versus-blind situation. The board is somewhat connected (8-J-7), making straights like T9 possible, but the river 2d is a complete blank. Flashlight's river bet of 1000 into a 2700 pot (approx. 37% pot) is a relatively small sizing that often indicates thin value or a blocking bet. Flashlight could be value betting a weaker Ace or even a high Jack, or potentially bluffing with missed straight draws. I will call to see the showdown.

My hand, 7c 8h, is a weak offsuit connector that does not play well in a 3-handed game. As the button, while I have position, I prefer to conserve my stack and wait for a more premium starting hand. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here.

In a blind-on-blind situation after the button folds, I have a semi-connected suited hand (10s7s). This is well within the range for an open-raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

T4o is a very weak starting hand. Defending it from the big blind against a 3x raise, even in a blind vs blind scenario, is highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with Jh 8d. This is a mediocre hand, but in a three-handed game, it's often worth a raise to try and take the blinds or play in position. I have 52o, one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With Flash folding on the button, I am in the Small Blind facing the Big Blind. Even for a steal or a limp, this hand is far too weak to play. Folding is the only sensible action here.
I am on the Button with 9d 5h, which is a very weak, unplayable hand in this position. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Flashlight, in the Small Blind, has completed the bet, meaning there is no additional amount for me to call. The flop is [Js, Kc, 8h]. My hand [Qc, 4s] has missed the board entirely, offering no pair, no draw, and effectively no showdown value. Checking is the optimal defensive play to keep the pot small and avoid unnecessary risk with air.

I have Ace-high (Ac 3d) on a board of Js-Kc-8h. Flashlight, who completed from the Small Blind, has checked to me. The board is paired with eights and my Queen-high has no showdown value or draw potential. Since the opponent checked the turn after checking the flop, they likely have a marginal hand or a weak range. Betting here is unlikely to force a fold from anything that has showdown value, and I have very little equity to improve. Checking back is the most disciplined play to control the pot size and take a free card to the river, avoiding unnecessary risk with a hand that is effectively air.

On a board of Js-Kc-8h-8d, my Ace-high still has some showdown value. I will check back to take a free river and hopefully get to a cheap showdown.

The board of Jack-King-8-8-9 offers no improvement to my Queen-high hand. Throughout the hand, both my opponent and I have checked every street, indicating a passive, likely low-value range for both. Betting now would be a bluff that is unlikely to fold out a pair or better. Checking is the most logical and conservative play to reach showdown and see if my high card is enough to win the pot.

In a limped blind-versus-blind pot, my Ace-high (Ac 3d) retains significant showdown value after both players have checked through the flop and turn. The river 9h doesn't drastically change the board texture for a limped range. Since Flashlight has checked for the third time, I have no reason to bet as I am unlikely to get called by worse or fold out a better hand. I will check back to realize my showdown value.

Nine-five offsuit is an extremely weak hand with poor playability and low equity. Even with button position, investing 100 chips into this pot is negative expected value. Folding is the most prudent and disciplined choice to conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 2s, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight (Button) has folded, leaving just me and Pro (Big Blind). I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop. Given my stack size of 72 BBs and the fact that I am first to act, there is no reason to enter the pot or attempt a steal with such low equity against the remaining players.

T7o is a weak hand to play from the small blind, especially against the big blind who has positional advantage and a large stack. The most profitable play over the long run with this holding is simply to fold and wait for a better spot.

Given the stack depths and the fact that I'd be sandwiching myself between an aggressive chip leader and the original raiser, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

While A5o is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal from the Button, it performs very poorly against a 3-bet. I am on the button with a suited hand (5c, Tc). In a three-handed game, the button is a prime position to open and attempt to steal the blinds. A standard raise is appropriate here to apply pressure to the small and big blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 8d, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight, the chip leader, has raised from the button to 300. Given my poor hand strength and the fact that I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against an aggressive chip leader, there is no reason to continue. I opened with 5c 10c from the button, a standard steal attempt, but facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind indicates significant strength or an aggressive defense. I am on the Button with 5s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is a 3-handed game where opening ranges should be wider, 53o is not a hand worth playing from any position, especially with active opponents like Flashlight (the chip leader) and Pro. I will fold to preserve my stack.

With AKo in the small blind after the button folds, I have a premium hand. I want to raise for value, build the pot, and take the initiative going into the flop. 7c 2c is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While the pot odds are tempting, calling with such a dominated hand out of position against a small blind open is generally a losing play in the long run. I have a healthy stack and prefer to wait for a more playable range to contest pots.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with King-Ten offsuit, which is a solid broadway hand well within a standard Button opening range. I'll raise to 2.5x to apply pressure on the blinds and try to win the pot uncontested or play a heads-up pot in position.

I am in the small blind with 8-6 offsuit, which is a weak hand. The button (Pro) has opened to 250. Given that I am out of position for the remainder of the hand and my hand has very low equity against a standard opening range, calling is not profitable. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine suited (Kd 9d), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. The button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I have flopped top pair with a King and a Nine kicker on a relatively dry board of K-7-5. This allows Pro to continuation-bet with their wider range, including bluffs and weaker hands that might fold if I lead out. My hand is strong enough to check-call most bet sizes on this flop.

I flopped top pair with a decent kicker on a very dry and disconnected board. A continuation bet is standard here for value and to protect my equity against random overcards or gutshots. A smaller sizing, around 1/3 to 1/2 pot, is ideal to keep the Big Blind's calling range wide, including middle pairs, worse Kings, or ace-high floats.

I have flopped top pair with a King and a 9 kicker on a relatively dry board. Pro's small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot is standard and could represent a wide range of hands, including total bluffs, mid-pairs, or stronger Kings. At this point, there is no need to raise and risk bloating the pot out of position or folding out hands I currently beat. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, see a turn card, and keep Pro's range wide, including their bluffs.

On the turn 10c, the board remains relatively safe for my top pair, although some straight draws like QJ or J9 are now possible, and KT has overtaken me. Following the standard line of checking to the pre-flop aggressor after calling a flop bet, I will check here. Leading out would likely fold out bluffs and only get called by better hands or draws. By checking, I allow Pro to continue betting with a wider range, including bluffs and weaker Kings, and I can comfortably check-call a reasonably sized bet to keep the pot manageable.

The turn brings the Ten of Clubs, giving me top two pair (Kings and Tens). With Flash checking again, this is a clear value betting situation. The board is slightly more coordinated with club flush draws and straight draws (like 89 or J9) now possible, so betting protects my equity and extracts value from worse Kings (such as KQ, KJ, K9), pair-plus-draw combos, and sticky middle pairs. A bet of roughly two-thirds pot charges draws appropriately while keeping slightly weaker made hands in the pot.

I have top pair with a Nine kicker on a board of K-7-5-T. Pro opened from the button and has fired two streets. The Ten on the turn is a slightly coordinated card, bringing potential two-pair combos like KT or straight draws like QJ and J9. However, my hand is still very strong in a three-handed context. Pro could be betting a weaker King (K8, K6, etc.) or a semi-bluff with a draw. The pot odds are favorable (650 to win a total pot of 2250), requiring roughly 29% equity. Folding top pair here would be too exploitable. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands. I will call to see a river and potentially pick up a cheap showdown or catch a bluff.

On the river 2d, the board has not significantly changed since the turn. My top pair with a Nine kicker remains a strong medium-strength hand. Given the action so far—calling a pre-flop raise and two subsequent bets—the most prudent course of action is to check. This allows Pro, who has been the aggressor, to either check back with a hand I beat or potentially bluff. Betting myself would likely only be called by better hands (like AK, KQ, KJ, or KT) and would fold out the bluffs I am currently beating.

The river brings a completely blank 2 of diamonds, missing all potential flush and straight draws. My top two pair (Kings and Tens) remains incredibly strong. Flash has check-called on both the flop and the turn, which heavily narrows his range to weaker Kings (like KQ, KJ, K9) or possibly stubborn middle pairs and missed draws. Since I beat virtually his entire calling range aside from extremely unlikely sets, a value bet is mandatory here. I will bet around half the pot to comfortably target his weaker Kx combinations, sizing it in a way that makes it difficult for a single top pair to fold.

On a board of 7-K-5-T-2, I hold top pair with a Nine kicker. My opponent (Pro) has bet three streets after opening from the button. In a three-handed game, top pair is a very strong hand and rarely folded, especially when the board is relatively disconnected. The Ten on the turn completed some gutshots like QJ, and I am losing to better Kings (AK, KQ, KJ) and two-pair combinations like KT. However, the pot odds are roughly 3.8-to-1 (I need to win about 26% of the time to break even), and Pro's range contains several missed draws (QJ, J9) and potential air bluffs. Given the wide ranges in short-handed play and the favorable price, I must call to catch potential bluffs, as folding here would be highly exploitable.

K-2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand, even in late position. As the chip leader, I do not need to take unnecessary risks with such a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack dominance and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. An Ace is a very strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to either win the pot immediately or take control of the hand with the range advantage provided by having an Ace.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-6 offsuit, facing a raise from the Small Blind. Despite getting 3:1 pot odds, 96o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and suffers from significant reverse implied odds. The best decision is to fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 10 of spades and 6 of spades. In a three-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. My standard opening raise has been 300, and I will continue with that sizing to maintain consistency and leverage my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 5s, a weak offsuit one-gapper. The Button raised to 3x the big blind. This hand does not have the equity or playability to call or 3-bet from out of position. I will fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h-3c, a very weak hand. Flash has raised to 300, and I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. Defending the big blind with such a low-value, offsuit hand is not statistically sound, especially against an aggressive opener. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am dealt 9-6 offsuit on the Button. This is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand with low equity. In a 3-handed game, the Button acts first pre-flop, and opening with such a weak hand is unprofitable. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand. With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. There is no significant advantage to playing this hand out of position, and completing the small blind to see a flop is typically poor EV with such a weak holding. I will fold and save my chips.

Furthermore, 22 plays terribly post-flop if it doesn't improve, and it blocks none of my opponent's value hands to make a 4-bet bluff viable. The most mathematically sound and disciplined choice is to fold.

With pocket eights on an As-Kc-2d-4h board, my hand has some showdown value against missed broadway cards or smaller pairs, but betting would mostly get called by better hands (like an Ace, a King, or a better pocket pair that checked the flop for pot control). Checking again allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate Flashlight's action. If he bets, I can decide whether his sizing looks like a bluff or if I should just fold my underpair.

I have middle pair (Kings) on an Ace-high board. Checking back is the standard play here as betting would only bloat the pot against hands that beat me (Aces) or make me pay to get called by hands I'm already beating. Checking allows me to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown.

With pocket eights on a board of As-Kc-2d-4h-7d and action checking through the flop and turn, my hand retains some decent showdown value against missed broadways (like QJ, QT) or smaller pocket pairs. However, a bet here is very unlikely to be called by worse and won't fold out any Ax or Kx hands that decided to pot control. Checking is the most solid play to get to a cheap showdown.

I am in the Big Blind and hold a Pair of Kings on a board of As-Kc-2d-4h-7d. Pro has checked the river, and the action is on me. Throughout the hand, both players have checked, indicating a lack of strong Aces or sets in either range. Betting now has minimal value, as I am unlikely to get called by a hand worse than mine, while betting into an Ace-high board against a player who has shown passivity carries unnecessary risk. Checking back allows me to take the hand to showdown and realize my equity safely, as my pair of Kings is a strong hand in this checked-down context.

Pre-flop action on the Button. I hold 5c 3d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. With two players left to act and poor card strength, the best and most standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit, a marginal hand. The Button (Pro) has folded. As the chip leader, I should look to apply pressure on the Big Blind (Flash), who has a significantly smaller stack. A standard raise from the small blind is an effective way to steal the blinds and potential antes, so I will raise to 300.

I am holding 9c 5d in the Big Blind. Flashlight, the massive chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I am holding 5-7 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with little potential for post-flop equity. Given that I have a significant stack lead and we are late in the tournament (hand 142/150), I should avoid playing marginal hands. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

I have Ace-Six offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a strong starting hand when the action folds to me in a blind-on-blind situation. A standard raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to either take down the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop play against Pro, while still maintaining some flexibility.

T7o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, who is the shortest stack and likely playing a reasonable range to protect or double up, folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Ten offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. With 24.25 big blinds, I need to look for opportunities to increase my stack as the tournament is nearing its end (hand 143 of 150). Raising is standard here to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately. A standard raise to 3x (300) is appropriate.

We hold pocket threes in the Small Blind. The Button, who is short-stacked with only 2125 chips, opens to 300. We don't have the implied odds to set-mine here because his remaining stack is only about 7 times the raise amount (ideally we want 10x-15x for set-mining to be profitable). A 3-bet jam is an option to pressure his wide Button opening range, but we risk being called by higher pairs or overcards where we'd be flipping at best, plus we still have the Big Blind behind us with a massive stack who could wake up with a real hand. Folding is the most prudent and mathematically sound decision.

I am in the big blind with 9s8s, which is a highly playable suited connector against a button raise. The pot odds are favorable (I need to call 200 into a 450 pot), and calling allows me to see a flop with good equity. Given my stack size and the opponent's aggressive tendencies, defending the big blind here is the standard and most strategic approach.

I have top pair (nines), an open-ended straight draw, and a flush draw on this board. This represents massive equity and a very strong range. Leading out with a bet allows me to build a pot while I am ahead or drawing, rather than giving my opponent a free card by checking.

On a flop of 6s-5s-9h, I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) but no direct draws and no spades. The Big Blind (Flashlight), who is the massive chip leader, has led into me for 400 (about 60% of the pot). This 'donk bet' typically indicates a pair or a strong draw on this board, which connects well with a Big Blind's defending range. Given my relatively short stack of approximately 18 big blinds remaining, calling 400 with just overcards and no real backup on a wet board is a poor use of resources. Q-high has very little showdown value here, and I would be putting myself in a difficult spot on the turn regardless of the card. I will fold and preserve my remaining 1825 chips for a better opportunity in the final hands of the tournament.

Q4s is a very weak hand, even on the button. With the Big Blind sitting on a roughly 20 BB stack and the Small Blind having a massive stack, a steal attempt here is very risky. The best move is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Deuce suited, which is a strong hand heads-up against the big blind. With a massive stack advantage over my opponent, I want to apply pressure and potentially take the pot down pre-flop or set up a favorable situation for the post-flop play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I have the nut flush draw on a flop with two clubs. This is a strong semi-bluffing opportunity. Betting now allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or build value if I hit my flush. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a standard continuation bet to apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind and have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker (Ts Jc) on a board of 4c-Tc-9s. I have the nut flush draw on a turn board of four clubs, ten, nine, and king. The pot is 1400 and I am the aggressor from the flop. On the turn, the King of Spades is a significant scare card that likely favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. With the tournament ending in only six hands, I need to preserve my remaining 14 BBs to find a more favorable spot for a double-up rather than risking my tournament life on a secondary pair against the massive chip leader.

Pocket threes is a playable hand in a 3-handed game, especially on the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define the range of the blinds. A standard raise size of 300 is appropriate to apply pressure.

I have 13.75 big blinds left and am the short stack with only six hands remaining in the tournament. Raising would be ill-advised as it turns a weak hand into a bluff against a 3-betting range that likely hits this board well. I will call the 50 chips to see the turn card cheaply.

K8o is too weak to play out of position against a button open and a small blind flat call. Calling here would put me in a marginal spot post-flop with a hand that plays poorly multiway. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

It is the final hand of the tournament, and I have a commanding chip lead. Qh-6d is a marginal hand, but as the small blind with a significant stack advantage, a raise is an aggressive play to pressure the big blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

A bet of 500 (one-third of the pot) is a standard continuation bet size in a 3-bet pot that keeps the pot manageable while still applying pressure.

The pot currently contains 2000 chips, and I am facing a bet of 500 chips. With a pair of Queens on an Ace-high board (A-Q-T), my hand is likely behind an Ace, but given the pot odds (4:1) and my commanding chip lead in this final hand, calling is the most rational decision. It allows me to see the turn card at a relatively low cost without committing further chips unnecessarily.

While the previous thought process was based on having only a pair of Queens, the current situation with two pair on a board of Th-Ad-Qd-6h is much stronger. However, Flashlight's lead on the turn for 1450 effectively puts me all-in. I have already committed 1250 chips to the pot, and I only have 1450 remaining. The pot is offering me nearly 3:1 odds (calling 1450 to win a total pot of 5400). Given my short stack and the strength of Top Pair in a three-handed game, I cannot fold here.
